summary: Jeff Atwood argues for minimizing third-party dependencies in software projects, drawing inspiration from the Excel team's famous motto 'Find the dependencies – and eliminate them.' He criticizes developers who compulsively adopt every new third-party control and framework, noting that deployment becomes a nightmare of license files and version management. While acknowledging the .NET Framework as a comprehensive base, he warns against stacking additional dependencies without clear justification. He balances this view by noting the Lava Flow anti-pattern but dismisses it as a non-issue for developers willing to aggressively delete unused code.
key_points: Third-party dependencies create deployment complexity through license management, version conflicts, and vendor inconsistencies
The Excel team's dependency-averse culture enabled them to ship on time with high-quality code and even build their own cross-platform compiler
Code outside the base framework should be 'guilty until proven innocent' – adopted only when offering significant, proven benefit
Aggressive code deletion prevents the Lava Flow anti-pattern where legacy code accumulates because no one understands it
The goal isn't zero dependencies but the smallest practical dependency stack
money_quotes: I think anything outside the base framework is guilty until proven innocent.
My code may suck, but it's a constant level of sucking that I can plan around.
Find the dependencies – and eliminate them.
When you're working on a really, really good team with great programmers, everybody else's code, frankly, is bug-infested garbage, and nobody else knows how to ship on time.
When you're a cordon bleu chef and you need fresh lavender, you grow it yourself instead of buying it in the farmers' market.
I am pathologically addicted to deleting code.
I don't care if it woulda, coulda, shoulda been used – is it being used right now? No? Then it's gone.
Strive to make your dependency stack as small as you possibly can.
key_insight: Every dependency you adopt is a liability you must maintain, so treat external code as guilty until proven innocent and keep your dependency stack as minimal as possible.
